seo.title: DOM Selection and Manipulation in JavaScript - getElementById, querySelector, Styles | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn how to select and manipulate HTML elements with JavaScript. Master getElementById, querySelector, querySelectorAll, textContent, innerHTML, setAttribute, classList, and element.style with 58+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: javascript dom manipulation, getElementById javascript, querySelector javascript, javascript change text, javascript change style, javascript classList, dom tutorial for beginners, javascript html manipulation, querySelectorAll, javascript setAttribute

content.what:
What Is the DOM?

The DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree-like representation of your HTML page that JavaScript can read and change. When a browser loads an HTML file, it builds a DOM tree where every HTML tag becomes a node (an object). JavaScript can then select these nodes, read their content, and modify them -- changing text, styles, attributes, and structure in real time.

Think of it like this: HTML is the blueprint of a house. The DOM is the actual built house. JavaScript is the person who can walk through the house and repaint walls, move furniture, or add new rooms.

<!-- This HTML... -->
<div id="container">
  <h1 class="title">Hello</h1>
  <p>Welcome to my page</p>
</div>

<!-- ...becomes this tree in the DOM:
  document
    html
      head
      body
        div#container
          h1.title  -> "Hello"
          p         -> "Welcome to my page"
-->

Every element in the DOM is an object with properties and methods. You can use JavaScript to find any element and change it.
content.why:
Why Learn DOM Selection and Manipulation?

1. It Is What Makes Websites Interactive

Without DOM manipulation, a webpage is just static text and images. Every button click, every form submission, every dropdown menu, every dark mode toggle -- all of these work because JavaScript selects elements and changes them.

2. It Connects HTML, CSS, and JavaScript

This is where the three languages come together. HTML gives structure, CSS gives style, and JavaScript uses the DOM to read and change both. Understanding the DOM is the bridge between knowing JavaScript and actually building real websites.

3. Every Framework Uses It

React, Vue, Angular -- all of these frameworks are built on top of DOM manipulation. They just hide it behind abstractions. If you understand the raw DOM, you will understand these frameworks much faster when you learn them later.

4. It Is Tested in Interviews and Exams

Questions like "What is the difference between textContent and innerHTML?" or "How do you add a class to an element?" are common in web development interviews. Knowing the DOM well gives you an edge.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Selecting Elements by ID

document.getElementById() returns the single element with the matching id. IDs must be unique on a page, so this always returns one element (or null if not found).

<h1 id="main-title">Welcome</h1>

<script>
const title = document.getElementById("main-title");
console.log(title);           // <h1 id="main-title">Welcome</h1>
console.log(title.textContent); // "Welcome"
</script>

2. Selecting with querySelector and querySelectorAll

document.querySelector() takes any CSS selector and returns the first matching element. document.querySelectorAll() returns all matching elements as a NodeList.

<ul>
  <li class="item">Apple</li>
  <li class="item">Banana</li>
  <li class="item active">Cherry</li>
</ul>

<script>
// First .item element
const first = document.querySelector(".item");
console.log(first.textContent); // "Apple"

// The element with both classes
const active = document.querySelector(".item.active");
console.log(active.textContent); // "Cherry"

// All .item elements
const allItems = document.querySelectorAll(".item");
console.log(allItems.length); // 3
allItems.forEach(item => console.log(item.textContent));
// Apple, Banana, Cherry
</script>

querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, not an array. You can use forEach on it, but not map or filter directly. To use array methods, convert it: Array.from(nodeList) or [...nodeList].

3. Changing Text Content

There are three ways to change the text inside an element:

  • textContent -- gets or sets the raw text. Ignores HTML tags.
  • innerText -- similar to textContent but respects CSS visibility (slower).
  • innerHTML -- gets or sets the HTML content. Parses HTML tags.
<p id="demo">Hello <strong>World</strong></p>

<script>
const p = document.getElementById("demo");

console.log(p.textContent); // "Hello World" (raw text, no tags)
console.log(p.innerHTML);   // "Hello <strong>World</strong>" (includes tags)

// Setting text
p.textContent = "New text";   // Replaces everything with plain text
// p is now: <p id="demo">New text</p>

// Setting HTML
p.innerHTML = "New <em>styled</em> text";
// p is now: <p id="demo">New <em>styled</em> text</p>
</script>

Security warning: Never use innerHTML with user input. If a user types <script>alert('hacked')</script>, innerHTML will execute it. This is called XSS (Cross-Site Scripting). Use textContent for user input.

4. Changing Attributes

HTML attributes (like src, href, alt, disabled) can be read and changed with JavaScript:

<img id="photo" src="old.jpg" alt="Old photo">
<a id="link" href="https://example.com">Click me</a>

<script>
const img = document.getElementById("photo");

// Read attributes
console.log(img.getAttribute("src"));  // "old.jpg"
console.log(img.getAttribute("alt"));  // "Old photo"

// Set attributes
img.setAttribute("src", "new.jpg");
img.setAttribute("alt", "New photo");

// Remove attributes
img.removeAttribute("alt");

// Check if attribute exists
console.log(img.hasAttribute("alt")); // false

// Direct property access (for standard attributes)
const link = document.getElementById("link");
console.log(link.href); // "https://example.com"
link.href = "https://google.com";
</script>

5. Changing Styles

You can change CSS styles directly with element.style.property. CSS property names use camelCase in JavaScript:

<p id="text">Styled text</p>

<script>
const text = document.getElementById("text");

// CSS: background-color  ->  JS: backgroundColor
text.style.color = "white";
text.style.backgroundColor = "#a855f7";
text.style.padding = "10px 20px";
text.style.borderRadius = "8px";
text.style.fontSize = "18px";
</script>

This adds inline styles. For toggling styles, it is much better to use CSS classes instead of setting styles directly.

6. Manipulating CSS Classes

The classList property gives you methods to add, remove, toggle, and check CSS classes:

<style>
  .highlight { background-color: yellow; font-weight: bold; }
  .large { font-size: 24px; }
  .hidden { display: none; }
</style>

<p id="text">Some text</p>

<script>
const text = document.getElementById("text");

// Add a class
text.classList.add("highlight");

// Add multiple classes
text.classList.add("highlight", "large");

// Remove a class
text.classList.remove("large");

// Toggle: adds if missing, removes if present
text.classList.toggle("hidden"); // Adds "hidden"
text.classList.toggle("hidden"); // Removes "hidden"

// Check if a class exists
console.log(text.classList.contains("highlight")); // true
console.log(text.classList.contains("large"));     // false
</script>

classList.toggle() is incredibly useful. It returns true if the class was added and false if it was removed.

7. Reading Input Values

To read what a user has typed into an input field, use the .value property:

<input type="text" id="name-input" value="Aarav">
<select id="city-select">
  <option value="delhi">Delhi</option>
  <option value="mumbai" selected>Mumbai</option>
</select>

<script>
const nameInput = document.getElementById("name-input");
console.log(nameInput.value); // "Aarav"

nameInput.value = "Priya"; // Change the input value

const citySelect = document.getElementById("city-select");
console.log(citySelect.value); // "mumbai"
</script>

8. Selecting Multiple Elements and Looping

When you need to change many elements at once, select them all and loop:

<ul id="fruits">
  <li>Apple</li>
  <li>Banana</li>
  <li>Cherry</li>
</ul>

<script>
const items = document.querySelectorAll("#fruits li");

// Add a class to every item
items.forEach(item => {
  item.classList.add("fruit-item");
});

// Change text of all items
items.forEach((item, index) => {
  item.textContent = `Fruit ${index + 1}: ${item.textContent}`;
});
// Now: "Fruit 1: Apple", "Fruit 2: Banana", "Fruit 3: Cherry"
</script>

codeExamples.title: Selecting and Changing Text
codeExamples.code: Hello, Guest!
codeExamples.explanation: When the button is clicked, JavaScript reads the value from the input field and updates the h1 text using textContent. The trim() call ensures we do not accept an empty or whitespace-only name.
codeExamples.output: A page with a heading that says 'Hello, Guest!' -- when you type a name and click the button, the heading changes to greet that name.

codeExamples.title: Toggling CSS Classes for Dark Mode
codeExamples.code:
My Website

This page supports dark mode.
codeExamples.explanation: classList.toggle("dark") adds the class if it is missing and removes it if it is present. The CSS transition makes the color change smooth. We also update the button text to reflect the current state using classList.contains().
codeExamples.output: Clicking the button switches between light and dark backgrounds. The button text updates to reflect the current mode.

codeExamples.title: Changing Attributes Dynamically
codeExamples.code: Image Switcher
codeExamples.explanation: Each button uses setAttribute() to change the image's src and alt attributes. This is how image galleries, sliders, and carousels work under the hood.
codeExamples.output: Three buttons switch which image is displayed by changing the src attribute of the img element.

codeExamples.title: querySelector with Different CSS Selectors
codeExamples.code:
Student List

  • Aarav
  • Priya
  • Rohan
  • Meera
codeExamples.explanation: querySelector accepts any valid CSS selector: IDs (#id), classes (.class), tags (li), attributes ([data-grade="A"]), compound selectors (.student.active), and pseudo-selectors (li:first-child). querySelectorAll returns all matches.
codeExamples.output: Displays: First li: Aarav, Active student: Rohan, Grade A students: 2, First in list: Aarav, Last in list: Meera

codeExamples.title: Inline Styles vs classList
codeExamples.code:
Aarav's Project Card

This card changes style when you click the buttons.
codeExamples.explanation: This example shows two approaches: classList.add/remove for switching predefined CSS themes, and element.style for setting inline styles. The classList approach is cleaner because styles are defined in CSS. Inline styles override class styles, which is why the red border appears even with a theme class.
codeExamples.output: Buttons switch the card between purple theme, teal theme, plain, or add a red border via inline style.

codeExamples.title: Reading and Displaying Input Values
codeExamples.code: Student Profile Preview
codeExamples.explanation: We read values from text inputs and select elements using .value. The preview div is updated using innerHTML (for bold formatting) or textContent (for plain error messages). The && check ensures all fields are filled before displaying.
codeExamples.output: A form with name, age, and city fields. Clicking 'Show Preview' displays the entered data below the form.

codeExamples.title: Looping Over querySelectorAll Results
codeExamples.code:
Task List

Complete maths homework
Practice JavaScript DOM
Read science chapter
Revise English essay
codeExamples.explanation: We use querySelectorAll(".task") to get all task elements, then forEach to loop through them. Each task gets a click handler that toggles the "done" class. The "Mark All" and "Clear All" buttons loop through all tasks and add or remove the class.
codeExamples.output: A list of tasks. Clicking a task toggles its done state. Buttons mark all as done or clear all.

commonMistakes.title: Using getElementById with a # Symbol
commonMistakes.error: Returns null because no element has the literal id '#main-title'.
commonMistakes.explanation: getElementById expects just the ID value, without the # symbol. The # is only used in CSS selectors and querySelector. This is a very common mistake.

commonMistakes.title: Trying to Use forEach on getElementsByClassName
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
const items = document.getElementsByClassName("item");
items.forEach(item => console.log(item)); // TypeError!
commonMistakes.error: TypeError: items.forEach is not a function. getElementsByClassName returns an HTMLCollection, not a NodeList.
commonMistakes.explanation: getElementsByClassName returns an HTMLCollection, which does not have forEach. Use querySelectorAll instead (returns a NodeList with forEach), or convert with Array.from().

commonMistakes.title: Using CSS Property Names Instead of camelCase in JS
commonMistakes.error: SyntaxError: JavaScript interprets the hyphen as a minus sign.
commonMistakes.explanation: CSS uses hyphens (background-color) but JavaScript uses camelCase (backgroundColor). Remove the hyphen and capitalize the next letter: font-size becomes fontSize, border-radius becomes borderRadius.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting that querySelector Returns Only the First Match
commonMistakes.error: querySelector returns one element, not a collection. There is no .length property on a single element.
commonMistakes.explanation: querySelector returns the first matching element. querySelectorAll returns all matching elements as a NodeList. If you need multiple elements, always use querySelectorAll.

commonMistakes.title: Script Runs Before HTML Elements Exist
commonMistakes.wrongCode: Old Title
commonMistakes.error: TypeError: Cannot set properties of null (reading 'textContent'). The element does not exist yet when the script runs.
commonMistakes.correctCode: Old Title
commonMistakes.explanation: The browser reads HTML top to bottom. If your script is in the head, the body elements do not exist yet. Either place your script at the end of the body, or wrap it in a DOMContentLoaded event listener.

summary: The DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree of objects representing your HTML page. JavaScript can read, change, add, and remove any part of it.
document.getElementById('id') selects one element by its ID. Do not include the # symbol -- that is only for CSS selectors.
document.querySelector('.class') selects the first element matching a CSS selector. document.querySelectorAll('.class') selects all matches and returns a NodeList.
textContent gets or sets plain text. innerHTML gets or sets HTML content (can parse tags). Never use innerHTML with user input due to XSS security risks.
setAttribute(), getAttribute(), removeAttribute(), and hasAttribute() let you read and change HTML attributes like src, href, alt, and data-* attributes.
element.style.propertyName sets inline CSS styles. Use camelCase in JavaScript: background-color becomes backgroundColor, font-size becomes fontSize.
element.classList provides add(), remove(), toggle(), and contains() methods for managing CSS classes. This is the preferred way to change styles.
The .value property reads or sets the current value of input, textarea, and select elements. This is how you get what the user has typed.
querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, not an array. You can use forEach on it, but for map/filter, convert it with Array.from() or the spread operator.
Always place your script at the end of the body or use DOMContentLoaded to ensure HTML elements exist before your JavaScript tries to select them.

topicSpecific.hint: textContent returns the text inside the element.
topicSpecific.explanation: getElementById("title") selects the h1 element. textContent returns the plain text inside it, which is "Hello World".

topicSpecific.hint: textContent strips HTML tags. innerHTML preserves them.
topicSpecific.explanation: textContent returns the raw text without any HTML tags: "Good Morning". innerHTML returns the full HTML content including tags: "Good <strong>Morning</strong>".
There are three <li> elements with class "item". querySelectorAll(".item") selects all of them, and .length returns 3.

topicSpecific.hint: The .value property reads and sets the current value of an input.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially the input's value is "Aarav". After setting input.value = "Priya", the value changes. Both the displayed text in the input field and the property update.

topicSpecific.hint: classList.contains() checks if an element has a specific class.
topicSpecific.explanation: The div has classes "card" and "active". contains("active") returns true because the class exists. contains("hidden") returns false because it does not.

topicSpecific.hint: Setting textContent replaces the element's text.
topicSpecific.explanation: We set p.textContent = "Updated", which replaces "Original" with "Updated". The console.log confirms the new value.

topicSpecific.hint: toggle() returns true if the class was added, false if removed. className returns all classes as a string.
topicSpecific.explanation: First toggle: "active" was not present, so it is added. Returns true. className is "card active". Second toggle: "active" is present, so it is removed. Returns false. className is "card".

topicSpecific.hint: getAttribute reads, setAttribute writes, hasAttribute checks existence.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially alt is "A cat". After setAttribute, alt becomes "A dog". The img has no title attribute, so hasAttribute("title") returns false.

topicSpecific.hint: innerHTML parses and renders HTML tags.
topicSpecific.answer: The h1 displays "Hello World" -- the word "Hello" in default color and "World" in red.
topicSpecific.explanation: innerHTML parses the HTML string. The <span> tag is rendered as an actual span element with red text. The h1 now contains two nodes: the text "Hello " and a red span with "World".

topicSpecific.hint: querySelector returns the first match. querySelectorAll returns all. NodeList is zero-indexed.
topicSpecific.explanation: querySelector returns the first li: "One". querySelectorAll returns all three li elements (length 3). Index 2 is the third element: "Three".

topicSpecific.hint: element.style only reads inline styles, not styles from a stylesheet.
topicSpecific.explanation: We set color, backgroundColor, and padding as inline styles. Reading them back works. But margin was never set as an inline style, so box.style.margin returns an empty string. Note: element.style only reads inline styles, not CSS file styles.

topicSpecific.hint: textContent does not parse HTML. It treats everything as plain text.
topicSpecific.explanation: When you set textContent, the string is treated as plain text, not HTML. The tags are displayed as literal text on the page. Internally, the browser escapes the angle brackets in innerHTML, showing the encoded version.

topicSpecific.hint: Attribute selectors filter by attribute value.
topicSpecific.explanation: The selector .box[data-color="red"] matches elements with class "box" AND data-color="red". Two elements match (A and C). The forEach logs their text content.

topicSpecific.hint: querySelector returns null when no element matches the selector.
topicSpecific.explanation: .text.highlight matches the element with both classes: the second p with text "Second". .text.missing matches nothing because no element has both "text" and "missing" classes, so querySelector returns null.

topicSpecific.hint: Changing the span's text changes it inside the parent too.
topicSpecific.explanation: We changed the span's textContent to "Aarav". The parent p's textContent returns all nested text: "Name: Aarav". The parent's innerHTML shows the span tag is still there with the updated text inside.

topicSpecific.hint: Track the classes step by step. add/remove can take multiple arguments.
topicSpecific.explanation: Start: "card". After add("active", "large"): "card active large". After remove("card", "large"): "active". Toggle "hidden" (adds it): "active hidden". Toggle "active" (removes it): "hidden".

topicSpecific.hint: The selected attribute determines the initial value. selectedIndex is zero-based.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially, "green" is selected (index 1). After setting value to "blue", the select changes to blue (index 2). selectedIndex is 0-based: red=0, green=1, blue=2.

topicSpecific.hint: textContent gives the visible text. getAttribute reads the attribute value.
topicSpecific.explanation: The anchor's visible text is "Visit" (textContent). The href attribute value is "https://example.com" (getAttribute).

mixed.hint: The code runs immediately, not on click.
mixed.answer: The button immediately shows "Clicked!" with a green background and white text. No click is needed because the code runs as soon as the page loads.
mixed.explanation: This code does not set up a click handler. It runs immediately and changes the button's text to "Clicked!", background to green, and text color to white. The button appears already changed when the page loads.

mixed.hint: Both methods select the same element.
mixed.explanation: Both querySelector("#box") and getElementById("box") select the exact same DOM element. Since they reference the same object, === returns true.

mixed.hint: Array.from converts a NodeList to an array, enabling map().
mixed.explanation: querySelectorAll returns a NodeList. Array.from converts it to an array. map() transforms each element's textContent to uppercase. The result is an array of uppercase strings.

mixed.hint: Adding a class that already exists does nothing. Toggle removes if present, adds if missing.
mixed.explanation: Start: "card". Add "active": "card active". Add "active" again: no change (already present). Toggle "card": removes it (was present). Toggle "new": adds it (was missing). Final: "active new".

mixed.hint: Checkboxes have a checked property (boolean) and a value property.
mixed.explanation: The checkbox is unchecked by default, so checked is false. The default value of a checkbox (when no value attribute is set) is "on". After setting checked = true, the checkbox becomes checked.

mixed.hint: forEach on a NodeList provides the element and its index.
mixed.explanation: forEach loops through each li element. The callback receives the element and its index. We build a string with the index and text of each item, then trim the trailing space.

mixed.hint: innerHTML destroys old elements and creates new ones. The old reference still points to the old element.
mixed.explanation: Setting innerHTML destroys the old p element and creates a new one. The variable inner still references the old (now detached) element with text "Hello". innerAgain references the new element with text "Replaced". They are different objects.

mixed.hint: Setting style.cssText replaces ALL inline styles with the new string.
mixed.explanation: Initially, color is "red" and fontSize is "20px" from inline styles. Setting style.cssText replaces the entire inline style. After the replacement, only "background: blue" remains. Color is now an empty string because it was removed.

mixed.question: What is the difference between textContent and innerHTML?
mixed.hint: One treats the content as plain text, the other as HTML.
mixed.answer: textContent gets or sets the plain text inside an element. It ignores HTML tags and treats everything as text. innerHTML gets or sets the HTML content, parsing and rendering any HTML tags. For security, use textContent when dealing with user input to prevent XSS attacks.
mixed.explanation: Example: setting el.textContent = "<b>Bold</b>" displays the literal text <b>Bold</b> on the page. Setting el.innerHTML = "<b>Bold</b>" renders the word Bold in bold.

mixed.question: Why should you avoid using innerHTML with user input?
mixed.hint: Think about what happens if a user types a script tag.
mixed.answer: Using innerHTML with user input creates a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability. If a user enters <img src=x onerror="alert('hacked')">, innerHTML will parse it as real HTML and execute the JavaScript. Always use textContent for user input, which treats everything as safe plain text.
mixed.explanation: XSS is one of the most common web security vulnerabilities. Attackers can steal cookies, redirect users, or deface websites. The simple fix is: use textContent for user-provided data, and only use innerHTML with trusted, developer-controlled content.

mixed.question: What is the difference between querySelector and getElementById?
mixed.hint: Think about what selectors each one accepts.
mixed.answer: getElementById only selects by ID and takes the ID string without the # symbol. querySelector accepts any CSS selector (#id, .class, tag, [attribute], combinations). getElementById is slightly faster for ID lookups, but querySelector is more flexible and is the modern approach.
mixed.explanation: Both getElementById("title") and querySelector("#title") select the same element. But querySelector can also do querySelector(".card.active"), querySelector("ul li:first-child"), and other complex selectors that getElementById cannot.

mixed.hint: classList is a DOMTokenList, not an array. Spreading converts it.
mixed.explanation: classList is a DOMTokenList object. Spreading it with [...p.classList] creates a real array. typeof returns "object" for both arrays and DOMTokenLists. Array.isArray correctly distinguishes them.

mcqs.question: Which method selects an element by its ID?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. document.getElementById() selects a single element by its unique ID. Option A uses CSS selectors. Option C selects by class name. Option D does not exist.

mcqs.question: What does document.querySelectorAll('.item') return?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. querySelectorAll returns a NodeList containing all matching elements. A NodeList is array-like but not a true array. It supports forEach but not map or filter directly.

mcqs.question: Which property should you use to safely display user input on a page?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. textContent treats the input as plain text and does not parse HTML tags, preventing XSS attacks. innerHTML would parse and execute any HTML or scripts in the input.

mcqs.question: How do you read the current value of a text input field?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. The .value property reads and sets the current value of input, textarea, and select elements. textContent and innerHTML are for reading content of regular HTML elements, not form inputs.

mcqs.question: What does getElementById return if no element matches?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. getElementById returns null when no element with that ID exists. This is important to check before using the element to avoid TypeError.

mcqs.question: How do you write background-color in JavaScript's style property?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. CSS property names with hyphens are converted to camelCase in JavaScript. Remove the hyphen and capitalize the next letter: background-color becomes backgroundColor.

mcqs.question: What does classList.toggle('active') do?

options.C: Adds the class if missing, removes it if present

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. toggle() adds the class if it is not present and removes it if it is. It returns true if the class was added and false if it was removed.

mcqs.question: Which selector would match <li class='item active'> but NOT <li class='item'>?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. .item.active (no space) selects elements with BOTH classes. .item .active (with space) selects .active elements that are descendants of .item elements. Option A matches both because both have class 'item'.

mcqs.question: What is the security risk of using innerHTML with user input?

options.B: It can cause Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) occurs when user input containing malicious HTML or JavaScript is inserted into the page via innerHTML. The browser executes the injected code, which can steal data or hijack sessions.

mcqs.question: How do you convert a NodeList to a real array?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Array.from(nodeList) and the spread operator [...nodeList] both convert a NodeList to a real array, enabling methods like map, filter, and reduce.

mcqs.question: What does element.style.fontSize read?

options.A: The font-size from any CSS source
options.B: The font-size from the stylesheet only
options.C: The font-size set as an inline style only

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. element.style only reads inline styles (styles set directly on the element via the style attribute or JS). To get the computed style from all CSS sources, use getComputedStyle(element).

mcqs.question: What happens when you set element.innerHTML = ''?

options.A: The element is removed from the DOM
options.B: All child elements inside are removed

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Setting innerHTML to an empty string removes all child nodes (elements and text) inside the element. The element itself remains in the DOM, just empty.

mcqs.question: What does setAttribute('disabled', '') do to a button?

options.A: Nothing, because the value is empty
options.C: Disables the button (boolean attributes are enabled by their presence)

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. HTML boolean attributes like disabled, checked, and readonly are active simply by being present on the element. The value does not matter. Even disabled="" disables the button.

mcqs.question: Which method returns the styles actually applied to an element (including styles from CSS files)?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. getComputedStyle(element) returns the final computed values of all CSS properties, including those from stylesheets, inherited styles, and browser defaults. element.style only reads inline styles.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between removeAttribute('disabled') and element.disabled = false?

options.B: removeAttribute removes the HTML attribute; .disabled = false sets the property to false. Both enable the element.

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. removeAttribute removes the attribute from the HTML markup. Setting the property to false changes the element's behavior. Both effectively enable the element, but they work at different levels (attribute vs. property).

mcqs.question: Which selector selects the first child <li> inside #list?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. querySelector returns the first matching element. #list li matches all li elements inside #list, but querySelector only returns the first one.

mcqs.question: What does element.className return?

options.C: A string of all class names separated by spaces

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. className returns a string like "card active large". For individual class manipulation, use classList which provides add, remove, toggle, and contains methods.

mcqs.question: Why should you place <script> tags at the end of the <body>?

options.B: It ensures all HTML elements exist before the script tries to access them
options.C: It is required by the HTML specification

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The browser parses HTML top to bottom. If a script in the <head> tries to access an element in the body, the element does not exist yet and getElementById returns null. Placing scripts at the end ensures all elements are loaded.

codingChallenges.title: Live Character Counter
codingChallenges.description: Create an HTML page with a textarea and a paragraph below it. As the user types in the textarea, show the character count in the paragraph. Example: 'Characters: 15'.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: (No input required -- type in the textarea)
codingChallenges.constraints: Use the 'input' event on the textarea and textContent to update the count.
codingChallenges.solution: Characters: 0

codingChallenges.title: Profile Card Builder
codingChallenges.description: Create a form with fields for name, age, and hobby. When a 'Generate Card' button is clicked, display a styled card below the form showing the entered information. Use textContent for security.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A card appears showing: Name: Aarav, Age: 15, Hobby: Coding
codingChallenges.constraints: Use getElementById to get input values. Use textContent (not innerHTML) to display user input.

codingChallenges.title: Color Theme Switcher
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with three buttons: 'Purple', 'Teal', 'Amber'. Clicking each button should change the page's background color and text color using classList. Define three CSS classes (.theme-purple, .theme-teal, .theme-amber) with different color schemes. Only one theme should be active at a time.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Background and text colors change based on which button is clicked.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use classList.add and classList.remove. Do not use inline styles. Remove the previous theme class before adding the new one.
codingChallenges.solution: Theme Switcher

codingChallenges.title: Student List Filter
codingChallenges.description: Create an HTML page with a list of 6 student names (as li elements with a class 'student'). Add a text input at the top. As the user types, hide students whose names do not match the search text (case-insensitive). Use classList to add/remove a 'hidden' class.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Only 'Priya' and 'Pramod' remain visible. Other students are hidden.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use querySelectorAll, forEach, textContent.toLowerCase(), and classList.toggle or add/remove.
codingChallenges.solution:
• Aarav
  • Priya
  • Rohan
  • Meera
  • Pramod
  • Kavya

codingChallenges.title: Attribute Inspector
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with an image element that has src, alt, width, and a data-photographer attribute. Add four buttons: 'Show src', 'Show alt', 'Change alt', 'Remove data-photographer'. Each button should perform its action and display the result in a paragraph below using getAttribute, setAttribute, and removeAttribute.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Clicking 'Show src' displays the image source URL. Clicking 'Remove data-photographer' removes that attribute.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use getAttribute, setAttribute, removeAttribute, and hasAttribute.

codingChallenges.title: Interactive Grade Card
codingChallenges.description: Build a grade card page. Create a table with 5 subjects (Maths, Science, English, Hindi, Computer) and input fields for marks. Add a 'Calculate' button. When clicked: (1) Read all marks from inputs. (2) Calculate total and percentage. (3) Display results below the table. (4) If percentage >= 90, add a 'distinction' class to the result div (gold background). If >= 60, add 'pass' class (green). Otherwise add 'fail' class (red). Use classList for styling.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use querySelectorAll to get all input values. Use classList for conditional styling. Do not use inline styles.
codingChallenges.solution:
Grade Card

Maths:
Science:
English:
Hindi:
Computer:

codingChallenges.title: Bulk Style Editor
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with 5 paragraph elements with class 'editable'. Add a control panel with: (1) a color input for text color, (2) a range input (12-36) for font size, (3) a checkbox for bold. As the user changes any control, ALL paragraphs should update their style immediately. Use querySelectorAll and forEach.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Set color to red, font size to 24, check bold
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: All 5 paragraphs turn red, 24px, bold simultaneously.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use querySelectorAll to get all paragraphs. Use the input event on controls. You may use inline styles here since the values are dynamic.
codingChallenges.solution:
Paragraph 1 by Aarav

Paragraph 2 by Priya

Paragraph 3 by Rohan

Paragraph 4 by Meera

Paragraph 5 by Kavya

codingChallenges.title: Accordion FAQ
codingChallenges.description: Build an FAQ accordion. Create 4 question-answer pairs where each question is in a div with class 'question' and the answer is in a div with class 'answer'. Initially all answers are hidden (display: none via a CSS class). Clicking a question toggles its answer visibility. Only one answer should be visible at a time -- clicking a new question hides the previously open answer.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Clicking 'What is JavaScript?' shows its answer and hides any other open answer.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use querySelectorAll, forEach, classList.toggle, and classList.remove. Do not use inline styles.
codingChallenges.solution:
FAQ

What is JavaScript?
JavaScript is a programming language that makes websites interactive.
What is the DOM?
The DOM is a tree of objects representing your HTML page.
What is querySelector?
querySelector selects the first element matching a CSS selector.
What is classList?
classList provides methods to add, remove, and toggle CSS classes.
